name: Kaumodaki Mace
description: The Kaumodaki is a powerful and divine mace, one of the principal weapons of the Hindu god Vishnu. It is often depicted as a heavy, club-like weapon adorned with intricate designs and imbued with immense power. The Kaumodaki is not merely a weapon; it symbolizes Vishnu's divine authority, strength, and the power to punish evil and maintain cosmic order. Its origins are often linked to the churning of the ocean of milk (Samudra Manthana), where it emerged along with other divine objects. The mace is said to possess the ability to crush enemies and obstacles with ease, representing Vishnu's unwavering commitment to protecting Dharma. The Kaumodaki is considered an integral part of Vishnu's iconography, often appearing alongside his other attributes like the Sudarshana Chakra (discus), Panchajanya (conch), and Padma (lotus). It represents the destructive aspect of Vishnu, used to vanquish demons and restore balance to the universe. The mace's weight and strength are legendary, signifying the overwhelming power of righteousness against unrighteousness. The Kaumodaki is a potent reminder of the divine protection and justice that Vishnu embodies. Its presence in Vishnu's hand signifies the constant battle between good and evil and the ultimate triumph of Dharma. The mace is revered by devotees as a symbol of divine power and protection, representing the ability to overcome adversity and achieve spiritual growth. Its imagery evokes a sense of awe and respect for the divine force that governs the cosmos.
subtitle: Vishnu's mighty Mace of Justice
origin: Emerged from the churning of the Ocean of Milk (Samudra Manthana)
currentLocation: Held by Vishnu in Vaikuntha or manifested in his avatars

keyMyths.title: Samudra Manthana
keyMyths.description: The Kaumodaki emerged from the churning of the Ocean of Milk, solidifying its divine origin and association with Vishnu.

keyMyths.title: Krishna's Battles
keyMyths.description: Krishna, an avatar of Vishnu, wielded the Kaumodaki in various battles against demons and evil forces, upholding Dharma.

sources.author: Swami Chinmayananda
sources.description: Commentary on the Bhagavad Gita, contextualizing Vishnu's role.
sources.text: Commentary on the Bhagavad Gita, contextualizing Vishnu's role.
